context: And go back to the book of Mark, if you will, we're gonna look at two passages of scripture in the gospels to prove the point that our Lord walked with compassion... And so notice, if you will, there in Mark chapter number one, pick it up with me in verse number 40. The Bible says this, and there came a leper to him, beseeching him and kneeling down to him and saying unto him,
point_summary: Christ's compassionate walk is illustrated through the leper of Mark 1:40, whose desperate, kneeling approach to Jesus serves as the opening scene for examining what it means to walk with compassion as Christ did.

context: if thou wilt, thou canst make me clean. I want you to notice several things about this leper. We're gonna look at verse 41 and 42 in just a moment as we deal with how Christ walked compassionately. But I want you to notice just a few things about this leper as the Bible tells us in verse number 40 that there was this leper. And of course, we understand the difficulty with leprosy and the problem during, especially in this particular time and what's going on. The Bible says that he came to Jesus, notice, beseeching him, begging, and kneeling down to him. And then notice what he says at the end of verse number 40. He says, if thou wilt, thou canst make me clean.
point_summary: The leper's approach to Christ—beseeching, kneeling, and saying "if thou wilt"—illustrates both faith in Christ's power to heal and submission to his sovereign will.

context: And the Bible says that Jesus is moved with compassion. He puts forth his hand and he simply says this after he touches him, by the way, that's amazing in of itself because you wouldn't touch a leper. And he touched him and he said, I will, be thou clean.
point_summary: Christ's compassion toward the leper is defined as a painful sympathy compounded of love and sorrow, moved by physical need and expressed through willing touch and immediate healing.

context: He was moved with compassion in Mark one.
point_summary: Mark 1 is read alongside Matthew 9:36–38 to establish that Jesus's compassion was moved by human need, and that this compassion—not mere duty—drove his purposeful engagement with the harvest.

context: Jesus was moved with compassion over a physical need.
point_summary: In Mark 1, Jesus is moved with compassion over a physical need, contrasted with Matthew 9 where his compassion responds to a spiritual need—people scattered as sheep without a shepherd.

reference_text: Go and preach the gospel to every creature
context: Go and preach the gospel to every creature
point_summary: While the timing of Christ's return remains unknown, believers have been given clear marching orders—including the command of Mark 16:15 to preach the gospel to every creature—to occupy faithfully until he comes.

context: and he said unto them, go ye into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature.
point_summary: The universal scope of the Great Commission — preaching to every creature — is presented as the biblical answer to partiality, countering ideologies that divide humanity by race or ethnicity.

context: he talks about the widow that puts in just two mites and he says she gave more than everyone else.
point_summary: God's evaluation of the widow's two mites illustrates that He measures giving not by visible amount but by the heart's motive behind the gift.

context: You read Mark chapter one and verse number 27, and you read how the demons obeyed God, why? Because he is the one with all authority and all power, and they are subject unto him.
point_summary: Creation, demons, wind, and waves all obey Christ because he holds all authority and power—making Saul's willful disobedience all the more inexcusable by contrast.

context: I want to remind you of what Jesus said in Mark chapter number 8
point_summary: Genuine discipleship, distinguished from self-serving seeking, requires willingness to bear the cross—a cost that separates true followers from those who pursue Jesus for lesser reasons.

context: And when he had called the people unto him with his disciples, also he said unto them, Whosoever will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me.
point_summary: Following Jesus and living for Jesus are synonymous, and genuine discipleship requires self-denial as the necessary first step before one can truly take up the cross and follow Christ.

context: whosoever will save his life shall lose it, but whosoever shall lose his life for my sake and the gospel's, the same shall save it
point_summary: Losing one's life for Christ and the gospel is the paradoxical condition of true discipleship, contrasted with the self-serving conflict that arises when a believer refuses to deny himself.

context: For what shall it profit a man if he shall gain the whole world and lose his own soul?
point_summary: Gaining the whole world at the cost of one's soul exposes the ultimate futility of following Jesus outwardly while refusing to deny self and bear the cross.

context: What shall a man give in exchange for his soul?
point_summary: Gaining the whole world while forfeiting one's soul exposes the ultimate worthlessness of any life not surrendered to Christ, framing self-denial as the only rational exchange.

context: Jesus, when he was in the midst of the storm, he was able to say, peace be still. And the winds and the waves obeyed and it was calm.
point_summary: Christ's authority over creation—illustrated by his calming the storm—is contrasted with human powerlessness to control circumstances, underscoring why only God can make and keep absolute promises.

context: we're familiar with the account in Mark chapter number 2, the man that was born of four
point_summary: The paralyzed man's four bearers illustrate that gospel work often requires collaborative effort, with no single laborer able to bring someone to Jesus alone.

context: whosoever will come after me
point_summary: Christ's call to deny self, take up one's cross, and follow Him defines the "crucified life" as the scriptural pattern for living unto Jesus rather than oneself.

context: let him deny himself and take up his cross and follow me.
point_summary: The "crucified life" of following Christ requires self-denial and cross-bearing, framed here as the pattern Jesus calls all people to, with eternal soul-loss as the ultimate cost of living for worldly gain instead.

context: For whosoever will save his life shall lose it, but whosoever shall lose his life for my sake and the gospels the same shall save it.
point_summary: Losing one's life for Christ's sake is the only path to saving it, contrasted against gaining the whole world while forfeiting one's soul for eternity.

context: For what shall it profit a man if he shall gain the whole world and lose his own soul?
point_summary: Gaining the entire world holds no ultimate profit if a person forfeits his soul, exposing the bankruptcy of living for worldly gain rather than for God.

context: What shall a man give in exchange for his soul?
point_summary: No earthly acquisition—wealth, status, or worldly success—holds any ultimate value if a person forfeits their soul for eternity.

context: Whosoever therefore shall be ashamed of me and of my words in this adulterous and sinful generation, of him also shall the Son of Man be ashamed when he cometh in the glory of his Father with the holy angels.
point_summary: Shame before Christ at His coming awaits those who, though living for worldly gain, are ashamed of Him and His words in this sinful generation.

context: So the Lord gives us this great pattern here in Mark chapter number 8 in verse 34, He gives us the, the progression, if you will.
point_summary: Genuine discipleship in following Jesus is not merely a children's song but a lifelong adult commitment, set against the warning that earthly gain without living for Christ leaves a believer empty-handed at the judgment seat.

context: if you don't follow the prescribed pattern that's set forth in Mark 8 in verse 34, you're going to struggle following Jesus
point_summary: Genuine discipleship follows a prescribed sequence in Mark 8:34—self-denial must precede cross-bearing and following Jesus, because attempting to follow Christ without first dying to self produces ongoing internal division.

context: The Bible tells us of hell that it's a place where the worm dieth not.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: In Mark chapter number 2, again, we don't have time to look through the entirety of the passage, but I want you to remember what's happening here in Mark chapter number 2 and give you some feed for thought, some looking at this passage maybe later and studying through it.
point_summary: In Mark 2, Jesus' declaration of forgiveness to the paralytic demonstrates divine authority, since forgiving sins is a prerogative belonging to God alone.

context: Immediately when Jesus perceived in the Spirit that they had so reasoned within themselves, He said unto them, Why reason ye these things in your hearts?
point_summary: Jesus's act of forgiving the paralyzed man's sins—claimed by the scribes as God's prerogative alone—serves as evidence that the Son of Man is God.

context: Whether is it easier to say to the sick of the palsy, Thy sins be forgiven thee, or to say, Arise and take up thy bed and walk?
point_summary: By healing the paralytic and forgiving his sins, Jesus implicitly claimed divine identity—since, as even the scribes acknowledged, only God can forgive sins.

context: But that ye may know that the Son of Man hath power on earth to forgive sins.
point_summary: Jesus's claim in Mark 2:10 to forgive sins—a prerogative the scribes correctly identified as belonging to God alone—is presented as direct evidence of His deity.

context: And then He turns to the man that was sick of the palsy. You got to see it, you got to understand what's happening. He's talking to these scribes that rightfully say, Who can forgive sins but God only? And He says this to the scribes, and then He looks to the man that's sick of the palsy, and He says to him, Arise, take up thy bed, and go thy way into thine house.
point_summary: By healing the paralytic after forgiving his sins, Jesus directly answered the scribes' own standard—only God forgives sins—proving His deity.

context: And here in Mark 16 and verse 15, He, Jesus said unto them, the disciples, go ye into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature.
point_summary: The Great Commission, recorded in Mark 16:15 as a single verse, gives the church its universal mandate to preach the gospel to every creature—a command the sermon frames as God's still-binding final charge.

context: He said unto them, Go ye into all the world, and preach the gospel to every creature.
point_summary: The Great Commission of Mark 16:15, repeated across all four Gospels and Acts, is not an optional consideration but a binding command that every believer is responsible to obey.

context: as we started this evening in Mark 16, is given in each of the four Gospels
point_summary: The Great Commission of Mark 16 encompasses local witness first, as illustrated by Acts 1:8's progression beginning in Jerusalem, making the congregation itself the missionary presence to its own community.

context: We need to be doing what God has called us to do, and of course this morning, bringing people to Jesus, looking at that passage in Mark chapter number two.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We find that the Bible says in Mark 16, preach the gospel to every creature.
point_summary: The universal scope of the Great Commission — preaching the gospel to every creature — is confirmed across Matthew 28, Mark 16:15, and Acts 1, establishing that the gospel is for all people without exception.

context: What was the instruction to go and preach the gospel to every creature?
point_summary: The disciples' upward gazing is rebuked by the angels precisely because Christ had already issued his marching orders—go, preach, witness—leaving no warrant for standing still.

context: And from thence he arose, and went into the borders of Tyre and Sidon, and entered into a house, and would have no man know it, but he could not be hid.
point_summary: Even in seeking seclusion, Jesus "could not be hid," a detail the sermon flags as a powerful truth about his person, though it reserves fuller treatment for another occasion.

context: For a certain woman, whose young daughter had an unclean spirit, heard of him, and came and fell at his feet.
point_summary: A Gentile woman's act of falling at Jesus' feet in desperate faith introduces the parallel Markan account of the same encounter recorded in Matthew, noting details unique to Mark's telling.

context: The woman was a Greek, a Syrophoenician by nation, and she besought him that he would cast forth the devil out of her daughter.
point_summary: Mark's identification of the woman as "a Greek, a Syrophoenician by nation" establishes her as a Gentile outsider whose persistent faith nonetheless moved Christ to heal her daughter.

context: for a certain woman whose young daughter had an unclean spirit, notice these three words, heard of him.
point_summary: A desperate Gentile mother's faith began with hearing—Mark records she "heard of him" without explaining how, underscoring that the manner of hearing matters less than the fact of it.

context: when it says that she heard of him and came and fell at his feet
point_summary: The Syrophoenician woman's act of falling at Jesus' feet in Mark 7:25 is presented as genuine worship, evidence that Jesus is God, since he—unlike angels—accepts rather than refuses such adoration.

context: In Mark's account in chapter number 7, we find that the Bible tells us in verse number 26 that she was a Greek, a Syrophoenician by nation, and she besought him that he would cast forth the devil out of her daughter.
point_summary: This Syrophoenician woman's desperate request to cast the devil from her daughter flows directly from her prior recognition of Christ's identity, worthiness of worship, and all-encompassing power.

context: We're told in verse number 25 that it was a young daughter.
point_summary: The Syrophoenician woman's desperate request for her daughter's deliverance was grounded in her prior recognition of Christ's identity, worthiness of worship, and sovereign power.

context: ...and He said unto her, for this saying, go thy way.
point_summary: Genuine heart transformation—not mere outward compliance—is the exclusive work of Jesus, illustrated here by His sovereign word alone expelling the demon from the Syrophoenician woman's daughter.

context: I'm mindful of what Jesus said in Mark chapter 8.
point_summary: Every child of God bears a general call to follow Jesus, illustrated here by Christ's words in Mark 8:34 addressed not only to the disciples but to the people gathered with them.

context: when He had called the people unto Him, with His disciples also, He said unto them
point_summary: The universal call to follow Christ—addressed not only to the apostles but to all people—demands self-denial and cross-bearing, marking discipleship as a serious, costly commitment for every child of God.

context: For whosoever will save his life shall lose it, but whosoever shall lose his life for my sake and the gospel's, the same shall save it
point_summary: Following Christ is a serious, universal call requiring self-denial and cross-bearing, as Jesus' "whosoever" in Mark 8:34–35 addresses not only disciples but all people.

context: For what shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world and lose his own soul?
point_summary: Gaining the whole world at the cost of one's soul is the ultimate proof that following Christ demands self-denial, because nothing a person can acquire equals the worth of the soul.

context: What shall a man give in exchange for his soul?
point_summary: No soul can afford the cost of following self over Christ, for what a man might gain in the whole world cannot compensate for the loss of his own soul.

context: Whosoever therefore shall be ashamed of me and of my words in this adulterous and sinful generation, of him also shall the Son of Man be ashamed when He cometh in the glory of His Father with the holy angels.
point_summary: Shame before Christ at His coming awaits those who are ashamed of Him in this life, framing self-denial and cross-bearing in Mark 8:34 as the necessary pattern for genuine discipleship.

context: But I want you to focus on verse number 34 when Jesus says, whosoever will come after me, notice what He says, let him deny himself, take up his cross, and follow me.
point_summary: Genuine discipleship, Jesus declares to the crowd and disciples alike, requires self-denial and cross-bearing as the necessary preconditions before one can truly follow Him.

context: I am not come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance.
point_summary: Christ's declaration that he came to call sinners, not the righteous, grounds the sermon's call to love what Jesus loves and pursue the lost with the gospel.

context: Master, carest thou not that we perish?
point_summary: The disciples' security amid the storm illustrates that God sovereignly permits difficulties in believers' lives, knowing the outcome, because trials reveal Christ's power in ways no other setting can.

context: Peace, be still.
point_summary: The disciples' encounter with the storm—and Christ's command "Peace, be still"—illustrates that God sovereignly permits difficulties in believers' lives, using them to reveal his power and teach lessons learned in no other setting.

context: And He said unto them, Go ye into all the world, and preach the gospel to every creature.
point_summary: God's promise to those who answer the Great Commission includes not only His presence but His protection, as the signs following believers in Mark 16 are here framed as divine assurance for those sent into the world.

context: He that believeth and is baptized shall be saved, but he that believeth not shall be damned.
point_summary: God's promise of protection accompanies the missionary call, with Mark 16:16–18's signs understood as authenticating apostolic authority in the New Testament era rather than a normative missionary guarantee.

context: And these signs shall follow them that believe. In my name shall they cast out devils. They shall speak with new tongues.
point_summary: God's promise of protection accompanies the missionary call, with Mark 16:17's signs understood within a dispensational framework as authenticating apostolic authority rather than as ongoing normative gifts.

context: They shall take up serpents. And if they drink any deadly thing, it shall not hurt them. They shall lay hands on the sick, and they shall recover.
point_summary: God's promise to those who answer the missionary call includes not only His presence but His protection, illustrated here through the sign gifts accompanying the apostles' proclamation in the early church.

context: He marveled because of their unbelief.
point_summary: Jesus marveled at unbelief (Mark 6:5–6), while here the religious leaders marvel in the opposite direction—at the unexpected boldness of Spirit-filled, unlearned men.

context: This morning as we travel to the book of Mark in the second chapter, we really take a journey
point_summary: Mark 2's account of bringing a paralyzed man to Jesus in Capernaum is introduced as a source of "valuable lessons" about bringing people to Christ, framed within a missions-conference series on spiritual preparation.

context: The Bible says in verse number one, and again, he, speaking of the Lord Jesus Christ, entered into Capernaum after some days.
point_summary: Christ's return to Capernaum drew such crowds that the house overflowed, setting the scene for God's plan to reach people through the gathered presence of Jesus and his word.

context: Notice verse number one continues and says, and it was noised that he, Christ, was in the house. And straightway many were gathered together in so much that there was no room to receive them.
point_summary: When crowds filled the house leaving no room even at the door, Christ preached the word to them — a detail that frames the entire passage around the priority of proclamation.

context: And the Bible says in verse number three, and they come unto him bringing one sick of the palsy, which was born of four.
point_summary: Four men's act of physically carrying and bringing a paralyzed man to Jesus illustrates the sermon's central theme that God uses his people to bring others to Christ.

context: whether is it easier to say to the sick of the palsy, thy sins be forgiven thee or to say, arise and take up thy bed and walk
point_summary: Jesus poses the question of which is easier—forgiving sins or commanding physical healing—to demonstrate that His authority to do both establishes His identity as the Son of Man with power on earth to forgive sins.

context: But that ye may know that the son of man hath power on earth to forgive sins
point_summary: Jesus's claim to forgive sins is presented as proof of his divine identity, directly answering the scribes' own logic that only God can forgive sin.

context: I say unto thee, arise, take up thy bed and go thy way into thine house
point_summary: Jesus's command to the paralyzed man to rise and walk served as public proof of His authority to forgive sins, validating His divine identity against the scribes' unspoken charge of blasphemy.

context: immediately he arose, took up the bed and went forth before them all
point_summary: The immediate, public healing in Mark 2:12 — witnessed by all and met with amazement and glorifying of God — serves as visible confirmation of Christ's authority to forgive sins.

context: like the man described in Mark chapter number two
point_summary: The paralyzed man's complete dependence on four friends to bring him to Jesus illustrates that sinners are spiritually helpless and require others, moved by genuine concern, to act on their behalf.

context: In fact, in verse number two, the Bible tells us that there were so many that gathered that there was no room to receive them. No, not so much as about the door.
point_summary: The crowd so packed the house that there was no room even at the door, establishing the physical obstacle the four friends had to overcome to reach Jesus.

context: According to verse number four, ...
point_summary: Undeterred by an impassable crowd, the four men's willingness to tear open a roof to reach Jesus demonstrates their unshakeable confidence that he could heal their friend.

context: whether is it easier in verse number nine to say to the sick of the palsy, Thy sins be forgiven thee, or to say, Arise and take up thy bed and walk?
point_summary: Jesus uses the question in verse 9 to expose that forgiving sins—not merely healing the body—is the deeper and more significant need of humanity, a power belonging to God alone.

context: But that ye may know that the Son of Man hath power on earth to forgive sins.
point_summary: Jesus's healing of the paralytic in verse 11 serves as visible proof of the claim in verse 10—that the Son of Man possesses authority on earth to forgive sins, a power belonging to God alone.

context: He then says to the man, Arise in verse number 11, Take up thy bed and go thy way into thine house.
point_summary: Jesus's command to the paralyzed man to rise and walk served as visible proof of his claimed authority to forgive sins, demonstrating that only God can do both.

context: Jesus saw their faith, and he said unto the sick of the palsy, son, thy sins be forgiven thee.
point_summary: Jesus's declaration "thy sins be forgiven thee" in Mark 2:5 is cited as evidence that he exercises a divine prerogative—since the scribes correctly recognized that only God can forgive sins.

context: there were certain scribes that were sitting there, and the Bible tells us in verse number six that they were reasoning in their own hearts.
point_summary: The scribes' silent accusation of blasphemy—"Who can forgive sins but God only?"—is treated as an unwitting confession that Jesus, in forgiving sin, was doing what only God can do.

context: Why doth this man thus speak blasphemies? Who can forgive sins but God only?
point_summary: Only God can forgive sins—a truth the scribes themselves affirmed when they charged Jesus with blasphemy for pronouncing forgiveness, thereby unwittingly testifying to His divine identity.

context: And immediately when Jesus perceived in the Spirit that they so reasoned within themselves...
point_summary: Jesus's perception of the scribes' unspoken reasoning demonstrates a divine omniscience that corroborates his authority to forgive sins—an authority the scribes themselves acknowledged belongs to God alone.

context: Whether it is easier to say to the sick of the palsy, thy sins be forgiven thee, or to say, arise and take up thy bed and walk.
point_summary: Jesus' authority to forgive sins—claimed in Mark 2:9's healing challenge—is presented as evidence of his deity, since the scribes rightly acknowledged that only God can forgive sins.

context: But that ye may know, and then notice the title, that the Son of Man...
point_summary: Jesus' claim in Mark 2:10 — that the Son of Man has power on earth to forgive sins — is presented as direct proof of his deity, since the scribes themselves acknowledged that only God can forgive sins.

context: but that ye may know that the Son of Man hath power on earth to forgive sins. So he's saying that to these religious leaders, to these scribes.
point_summary: Jesus' healing of the paralytic is presented as a deliberate, public demonstration to the scribes that the Son of Man possesses divine authority to forgive sins.

context: And then he turns his attention to the man that was sick of the palsy, and he said unto him, arise, take up thy bed, and go thy way into thine house.
point_summary: Jesus heals the paralyzed man as a deliberate, visible proof to the scribes that his authority to forgive sins—rightly understood as belonging to God alone—confirms his identity as Messiah and God.

context: And immediately he arose, took up the bed, and went forth before them all.
point_summary: The healing of the paralytic is presented as a deliberate, public proof that Jesus possesses divine authority to forgive sins, confirming His identity as Messiah and God.

context: And as He's standing there before the high priest, He's being questioned, and in verse number 61 of Mark 14, it says this. Mark 14 and verse 61, speaking of Jesus that He held His peace and answered nothing, again the high priest asked Him and said unto Him, Art thou the Christ, the Son of the Blessed?
point_summary: When Jesus answered the high priest's direct question "Art thou the Christ?" with "I am," the claim was understood as blasphemy—confirming that His divine self-identification was the explicit ground for His condemnation.

context: Jesus said, I am. I am. And ye shall see the Son of Man, that title that was referenced, sitting on the right hand of power and coming in the clouds of heaven.
point_summary: When asked directly if he is the Christ, Jesus answers "I am," a claim the high priest immediately condemns as blasphemy—confirming that Jesus was understood to be asserting full divine identity.

context: And some began to spit on Him and to cover His face and to buffet Him and to say unto Him, Prophesy. And the servants did strike Him with the palms of their hands.
point_summary: Jesus's omniscience as God is underscored by the claim that He knew the exact names of every person who struck Him during His trial before Caiaphas.

context: Caiaphas was beside himself when He asked Him plainly, Are you the Christ, the Son of the Blessed?
point_summary: When the high priest asked Jesus directly whether He was the Christ, the Son of the Blessed, Jesus answered "I am"—a claim the high priest immediately condemned as blasphemy, confirming Jesus was understood to be claiming deity.

context: Last week on Wednesday evening, we looked at that passage of Scripture in Mark chapter number 12, where those Sadducees, the title last week was, ye therefore do greatly err.
point_summary: The Sadducees' error in Mark 12, despite their scriptural familiarity, illustrates that true knowledge of Scripture is not mere mental aptitude but applied truth — ignorance of which leads to grave error.

context: He's not the God of the dead, but of the living.
point_summary: The Sadducees' denial of the resurrection is presented as the consequence of hearing Scripture without applying it — knowing the text mentally but not being doers of its truth.

context: And when he had called the people unto him with his disciples also, he said unto them, Whosoever will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me.
point_summary: Following Christ carries the cost of self-denial and cross-bearing, as Jesus himself warned his would-be followers that obedience to him would not be easy.

context: Go ye into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature.
point_summary: Obedience to God's generally revealed will—such as the command to preach the gospel to every creature—is the prerequisite condition under which He grants specific divine direction, as Paul's Macedonian vision illustrates.

context: And Jesus, answering them, began to say, Take heed, lest any man deceive you.
point_summary: Christ's opening command in Mark 13:5 — "Take heed, lest any man deceive you" — frames deception as a straying from the right way, with the Greek word carrying the sense of being led astray or aside.

context: For many shall come in my name, saying, I am Christ, and shall deceive many.
point_summary: Christ's own warning in Mark 13:6 frames the entire series: many will come claiming His name and identity, making deception not a peripheral danger but the very threat He commands His followers to guard against.

context: mark chapter number six I just want to look at verse five and six look at verse four with me It says this but Jesus said unto them a prophet is not without honor
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: but in his own country and among his own kin and his own house
point_summary: Jesus' saying that a prophet is without honor among his own people introduces a study on what caused Christ to marvel — specifically, the unbelief he encountered in his own country.

context: He couldn't could there do no mighty works save that he laid his hands upon a few sick folk and healed them
point_summary: Unbelief so constrained Jesus at Nazareth that even He marveled at it — a response the sermon treats as the launching point for a study of faith and unbelief in Christ's interactions throughout the Gospels.

context: And he marveled because of their unbelief And he went round about the villages Teaching
point_summary: Jesus's marveling at unbelief in Nazareth introduces a topical study contrasting the faith responses of various individuals Jesus encountered throughout his public ministry.

context: We just saw that same word in Mark chapter number 6
point_summary: Christ's marveling at unbelief in Nazareth (Mark 6:6) is set in deliberate contrast with his marveling at the centurion's great faith (Matthew 8:10), using the same word to frame two opposite responses that provoked his wonder.

context: in Mark chapter number 4 The record is given from Mark's perspective With regard to the same event and it's interesting for in Mark chapter number 4 The way in which Mark would record it is that he says here. Why are you so fearful? How is it that you have? no faith
point_summary: Mark 4's account of the storm is cited to show that where Matthew records Jesus rebuking "little faith," Mark intensifies the charge to "no faith" — illustrating a spectrum from no faith to great faith.

context: Back in Mark chapter number 6 and verses 5 and 6, we see that the Bible says in verse number 6, and he marveled because of their unbelief.
point_summary: Unbelief, likened to a weed that chokes out faith, is presented as something that must be recognized before it can be confessed and dealt with, as illustrated by Christ marveling at it in Mark 6:6.

context: Back in Mark chapter number 6 and verses 5 and 6, we see that the Bible says in verse number 6, and he marveled because of their unbelief.
point_summary: Jesus marveling at unbelief rather than faith serves as a sobering warning that unbelief, like a weed, must first be recognized before it can be confessed and dealt with.

context: Jesus says to him, if thou canst believe, all things are possible to him that believeth
point_summary: Recognizing unbelief begins with honest self-examination, illustrated by the father in Mark 9 who simultaneously confessed faith and acknowledged his own unbelief to Christ.

context: ...Notice what it said in verse number five of Mark 6, and he could there do no mighty works...
point_summary: Unbelief in one's own community produces a tangible result: the people of Nazareth missed out on witnessing God's mighty works because their lack of faith limited what Christ did among them.

context: Now the passage tells us here in Mark 6 and verse number 5, save that he laid his hands upon a few sick folk and healed them.
point_summary: Unbelief forfeits the fuller display of God's power, as both Mark and Matthew show that Nazareth missed mighty works they could have witnessed had they believed.

context: he marveled because of their unbelief
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: he marvelled because of their unbelief
context: would He marvel because of our unbelief
point_summary: Unbelief is presented as a universal struggle among God's people, with Mark 6:6's image of Jesus marveling at unbelief serving as a searching question about whether Christ would find faith or unbelief in the congregation today.

context: ...They don't understand Mark 16 and that's fine...
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: what shall it profit a man if he gained the whole world and loses his own soul?
point_summary: A believer's identity as a child of God surpasses all earthly titles and achievements, making that position in Christ the only gain that ultimately matters.

context: Probably the simplest form, the one that is most widely memorized is Mark 16 and 15, and we're to preach the gospel to every creature.
point_summary: Knowing the Great Commission by memory is not the same as actively fulfilling it; Mark 16:15's command to preach the gospel to every creature demands personal, ongoing obedience, beginning with one's immediate neighbors.

context: Go ye into all the world and preach the Gospel to every creature.
point_summary: The Great Commission's mandate to preach the Gospel to every creature carries divine authority, grounding present Christian conduct in the obligation to share the Gospel with urgency and enthusiasm.

context: He says it very plainly in the book of Mark, are you into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature.
point_summary: Every person stands accountable before God for the gospel command to go into all the world and preach to every creature, regardless of whether they have heard it preached.

context: we mentioned this morning in our Sunday school lesson as we looked at that man, that man that was possessed of a demon in Mark chapter number five
point_summary: The demon-possessed man's transformation in Mark 5 is cited as an illustration that genuine salvation produces a desire to be with Jesus and a changed life of obedience.

context: to go into all the world and preach the Gospel to every creature
point_summary: The universal mandate to preach the Gospel to every creature (Mark 16:15) is confirmed by Colossians 1:6's declaration that the Gospel has gone into all the world and bears fruit wherever it is proclaimed.

context: To just go forth and preach the Gospel to every creature.
point_summary: The Gospel's proven fruitfulness in the believer's own life grounds the confidence and obligation to preach it to every creature, trusting God's method to produce fruit in others.

context: And this is not the sister passage that deals with the same context of what we're dealing with with this question. We'll look at that in just a moment. But in Mark chapter number six, I want you to see this reference with regard to Herod. In Mark six and verse number 14, the Bible says this, and King Herod heard of him. The him is Jesus. It says, for his name was spread abroad.
point_summary: Popular confusion about Jesus's identity extended even to Herod, who wrongly concluded Jesus was John the Baptist risen from the dead, illustrating that widespread awareness of Jesus did not produce correct understanding of who he was.

context: Notice a few passages over in Mark chapter number nine, excuse me, Mark chapter number eight
point_summary: The parallel account in Mark 8:27–29 confirms that Jesus' question "Whom do men say that I am?" and the disciples' confused answers are not unique to Matthew but are corroborated across the Gospels.

context: in verse number 27, it says this, and Jesus went out and his disciples into the towns of Caesarea Philippi. And by the way, he asked his disciples saying unto them, whom do men say that I am?
point_summary: Mark 8:27 is cited as the Synoptic parallel to Matthew 16:13, confirming that Jesus' question about his identity at Caesarea Philippi is recorded across Matthew, Mark, and Luke as a singular, significant moment.

context: He'll then ask them in verse number 29. but whom say ye that I am?
point_summary: Mark 8:29's parallel phrasing of Jesus' direct question to the disciples — "but whom say ye that I am?" — confirms that Matthew, Mark, and Luke all record this pivotal moment as one of supreme significance.

context: this is my beloved Son in whom I am well pleased
point_summary: God the Father's declaration of love for the Son at Jesus's baptism—the first use of "love" in Matthew, Mark, and Luke—parallels the language of Genesis 22:2, where God calls Isaac Abraham's beloved son.

context: Jesus in Mark chapter number 4 he's in the ship and the Bible says that he was asleep in the in the hinder part the the back part of the ship why he was tired
point_summary: Cited as evidence of Christ's genuine humanity, Jesus sleeping in the stern of the ship demonstrates that he experienced physical weariness as a true man, while his subsequent calming of the storm reveals his concurrent deity.

context: he rebukes the wind and the waves and says peace be still and there's a calm
point_summary: Cited as evidence of Christ's true humanity, the fact that Jesus slept in the ship demonstrates he experienced genuine physical weariness, while his subsequent calming of the storm confirms his simultaneous deity.

context: master carest thou not that we perish
point_summary: Cited as evidence of Christ's genuine humanity, Jesus sleeping in the stern of the ship demonstrates that he experienced physical weariness as a true man, even while possessing divine power over wind and waves.

context: Jesus was there and he told this man that thy sins be forgiven in verse number five
point_summary: Jesus's declaration "thy sins be forgiven thee" in Mark 2:5 demonstrates His deity, since the scribes' own objection—"who can forgive sins but God only?"—confirms that forgiving sin is an exclusively divine prerogative.

context: those religious leaders the scribes in particular in verse number seven they say this who can forgive sins but God only
point_summary: The scribes' own words — "Who can forgive sins but God only?" — are used to establish that Jesus' act of forgiving sin is direct evidence of His deity.

context: Jesus says at the end of verse number eight why reason you these things in your hearts
point_summary: Jesus's response to the scribes' unspoken accusation in verse 8 is presented as evidence of His deity, since only God can perceive hidden thoughts and only God can forgive sins.

context: he says that you may know that the Son of Man hath power on earth to forgive sins
point_summary: Jesus's declaration in Mark 2:10 that the Son of Man has power on earth to forgive sins is presented as direct evidence of his deity, since the scribes themselves acknowledged that only God can forgive sins.

context: immediately in verse number twelve that man arose took up his bed and went forth
point_summary: The healing of the paralytic in Mark 2:12 is cited as evidence of Christ's deity, since the scribes' own objection—"who can forgive sins but God only?"—confirms that Jesus' authority to forgive and heal belongs to God alone.

context: the Bible says, the same day when the evening was come, he saith unto them, let us pass over unto the other side
point_summary: Christ's power over nature is demonstrated in his calming the storm with a word, rebuking the wind and sea to immediate stillness as evidence of his divine authority over the created order.

context: and the waves beat into the ship so that it was now full
point_summary: Christ's authority over nature is demonstrated in his commanding the storm to cease, proving to the terrified disciples—and to us—that he is God.

context: and he was in the hinder part of the ship, asleep on a pillow, and they awake him and say unto him, master, carest thou not that we perish?
point_summary: Christ's authority over nature is demonstrated in his commanding the storm to cease, prompting the disciples' awe-struck question about what manner of man could make wind and sea obey him.

context: And he arose and rebuked the wind and said unto the sea, peace, be still, and the wind ceased, and there was a great calm
point_summary: Christ's authority over the natural world is demonstrated in His single spoken command that instantly silenced the storm, prompting the disciples' awe-filled question about what manner of man He is.

context: and he said unto them, why are you so fearful? How is it that you have no faith?
point_summary: Christ's authority over nature is demonstrated in his rebuke of the storm, which silenced wind and sea instantly, prompting the disciples' awe-struck question about what manner of man he is.

context: And they feared exceedingly and said one to another, what manner of man is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?
point_summary: The disciples' awestruck question — "What manner of man is this?" — is answered directly: Jesus's command over wind and sea demonstrates that He is God.

context: Mark 16 and verse 20 says, that they went forth and preached everywhere, the Lord working with them and confirming the word with signs of following, amen.
point_summary: God's confirming work alongside the early disciples—working with them and validating the word through signs—grounds the Acts 4 prayer for boldness as an appeal to that same divine partnership in proclamation.

context: how be it Jesus suffered him not but saith unto him Go home to thy friends and tell them how great things the Lord hath done for thee and hath had compassion on Thee
point_summary: Jesus's command to the delivered man — "Go home to thy friends and tell them" — grounds the sermon's call to personal witness in the compassionate acts the Lord has done.

context: Jesus stands and says peace be still
point_summary: Christ's omnipotence is demonstrated, not merely declared, as his command "Peace, be still" over wind and sea in Mark 4:39 serves as one of several witnessed proofs of his power over nature.

context: He then says to them why are you so fearful how is it that you have no faith
point_summary: Faith's absence amid Christ's demonstrated power over nature is highlighted as the disciples' failure to trust the one whose authority the wind and sea obeyed.

context: they said one to another what manner of man is this that even the wind and the sea obey him
point_summary: Christ's omnipotence is not merely declared but demonstrated, as the disciples' awestruck question in Mark 4:41 shows the wind and sea obeying him, establishing his authority before the Gadarene encounter.

context: But when he saw Jesus afar off he ran and worshipped him
point_summary: Even demons recognize and confess the identity of Jesus as the Son of the Most High God, demonstrating that supernatural beings understand who he is even when men do not.

context: And cried with a loud voice and said what have I to do with thee Jesus now son of the Most High God I Adjure thee by God that thou torment me not
point_summary: Even the demons possessing the Gadarene man recognized and confessed Jesus as Son of the Most High God, demonstrating that demonic beings know precisely who Jesus is and fear His authority.

context: for he said unto him come out of the man thou unclean spirit
point_summary: Demons recognize Jesus as the Son of the Most High God and understand their own coming judgment, demonstrating that spiritual knowledge of Christ's identity does not equal saving faith.

context: he Jesus asked him What is? thy name and He answered saying my name is legion for we are many
point_summary: Demons recognize Jesus as the Son of the Most High God and understand their own coming judgment, demonstrating that spiritual knowledge of Christ does not equal saving faith.

context: And he besought him much that he would not send them away out of the country
point_summary: Demons recognize Christ's divine identity and understand their ultimate doom, knowing they face eternal torment—a recognition that surpasses the unbelief of many humans.

context: now There was there nigh unto the mountains a great herd of swine feeding
point_summary: Demons recognize Jesus as the Son of the Most High God and understand their ultimate doom, demonstrating that even Satan's forces possess knowledge of Christ's identity and authority.

context: and all the devils besought him saying send us into the swine that we may enter into them
point_summary: Demons recognize Jesus as the Son of the Most High God and understand their ultimate doom, demonstrating that even Satan's forces possess knowledge of Christ's identity and authority.

context: and forthwith Jesus gave them leave The unclean spirits went out and entered into the swine and the herd ran violently down a steep place into the sea there were about 2,000 and were choked in the sea
point_summary: Demons, knowing Christ's identity and fearing their destined torment, willingly submitted to Jesus' authority—entering swine and perishing—demonstrating that even unclean spirits cannot resist His command.

context: And They that fed the swine fled and told it in the city and in the country and they went out to see what it was That was done
point_summary: Demons recognize Christ's identity and understand the certainty of their own judgment, knowing their time is short and their ultimate torment in the lake of fire is assured.

context: And here in Mark 5, we're not told of the size of the crowd that returns, but we find that as they would return to the place in which these that had fed the swine told them of, that they came, and notice what it tells us in v. 15, and they come to Jesus, and notice what it says, and see Him that was possessed with the devil and had the legion.
point_summary: The dramatic contrast between the demoniac's former tormented state (vv. 3–5) and his appearance now sitting, clothed, and in his right mind testifies to the totality of the change Christ had worked in him.

context: And they come to Jesus, and see him that was possessed with the devil, and had the legion, sitting, and clothed, and in his right mind: and they were afraid.
point_summary: The dramatic contrast between the demoniac's former wildness (vv. 3–5) and his present state—sitting, clothed, and in his right mind—visibly demonstrates the transforming power of Christ's work in an individual life.

context: Who had his dwelling among the tombs; and no man could bind him, no, not with chains:
point_summary: The dramatic transformation of the demoniac in verse 15—sitting, clothed, and in his right mind—is set in deliberate contrast to the uncontrollable, tomb-dwelling condition described in verses 3–5.

context: Because that he had been often bound with fetters and chains, and the chains had been plucked asunder by him, and the fetters broken in pieces: neither could any man tame him.
point_summary: The dramatic transformation of the demoniac—from uncontrollable and unbound to sitting clothed and in his right mind—is presented as a deliberate contrast with the man's former condition described in verses 3–5.

context: And always, night and day, he was in the mountains, and in the tombs, crying, and cutting himself with stones.
point_summary: The dramatic contrast between the demoniac's former tormented state in verses 3–5 and his transformed condition in verse 15—sitting, clothed, and in his right mind—is presented as evidence of the great change Christ had wrought in him.

context: this man has a desire to depart to be with the Lord and Jesus suffered him not he allowed him not but he gave him some very important direction
point_summary: Commissioned to testify of God's great works and compassion, the healed man's calling illustrates that personal experience of redemption carries a responsibility to declare what the Lord has done.

reference_text: in Mark 5 that we're studying this evening
context: And the thing that's amazing is that in this particular account, in Mark 5 that we're studying this evening, the reality is that Jesus had not yet gone
point_summary: Sharing personal testimony of what Christ has done—illustrated by the healed man's witness in Mark 5—carries powerful evangelistic impact, even before the cross, resurrection, and ascension had occurred.

context: And Jesus suffered him not. He allowed him not. He said, no, you need to go home to your friends and you need to tell them, here's your commission.
point_summary: Conversion itself becomes the great thing God has done, making every believer—like the delivered demoniac—a commissioned representative sent to declare what Christ has accomplished.

context: Go home to thy friends and tell them how great things the Lord hath done for thee and hath showed compassion on thee.
point_summary: Urgency to witness is grounded in the present life being the only opportunity to obey Christ's command to "go home and tell thy friends," since no lost people exist in heaven to evangelize.

context: We thank you for this passage of Scripture in Mark 5.
point_summary: Mark 5's account of the delivered man being commissioned to "go and tell" is applied as the pattern for every believer's purpose: to declare publicly the great things God has done.

context: Go and preach the gospel to every creature
point_summary: The church's mandate to go forward is grounded in the command to preach the gospel to every creature, presented here as the marching orders that make retreat or stagnation disobedience.

context: the disciples would go to the Lord Jesus Christ and take and distribute that bread and that fish
point_summary: God's miraculous provision at the feeding of the five thousand — blessing, multiplying, and filling all — is cited as a faith-building memorial the disciples should have remembered when facing new need.

context: and we find that the multitudes would come to hear Him, they would hear as they did in Mark chapter number two, that it was noise, that He was in the city
point_summary: The crowds that filled the house in Mark 2 illustrate the magnetic authority of Christ's spoken word, reinforcing why neglecting to hear Him carries such serious danger.

context: What shall it profit a man if he gained the whole world but loses his own soul?
point_summary: Gaining the whole world while losing one's soul yields no profit, contrasting the emptiness of material ambition with the eternal life found in Christ.

context: and Jesus then says, peace be still, and the storm is calm.
point_summary: The disciples' storm in Mark 4 illustrates that trials can come precisely through obedience to God's direction, and that enduring them positions believers to witness divine power they would never otherwise see.

reference_text: What manner of man is this that even the winds and the waves obey him?
context: What manner of man is this that even the winds and the waves obey him?
point_summary: The disciples' storm, sent by Jesus' own command, illustrates that trials can come through obedience rather than sin, and that enduring them reveals God's power in ways that would otherwise remain unseen.

context: I believe, help thou mine unbelief.
point_summary: Honest, partial faith—"I believe, help thou mine unbelief"—is presented as a valid and recognizable posture before God, who actively works to build and increase that faith.

context: He said, go to thy friends and tell them how great things Christ hath done for you.
point_summary: Christ's command to the healed demoniac—go tell your friends what great things He has done—establishes that believers are left on earth to make Him known, not merely to observe prophetic fulfillment.

reference_text: Master, carest thou not that we perish
context: Master, carest thou not that we perish, right? Isn't that not what the disciples said?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Of course God cares, and all He had to do was say, Peace, be still.
point_summary: Christ's sovereign word over the storm — "Peace, be still" — is cited as evidence that God is greater than any problem, calling believers away from fear-driven focus on circumstances.

reference_text: What manner of man is this?
context: And all of their jaws were like, What manner of man is this?
point_summary: The disciples' awestruck question "What manner of man is this?" is cited as the natural response to witnessing God's power over the storm, illustrating that God alone is greater than any problem or crisis we face.

context: mark chapter number six. And specifically this evening, we'll be looking at verses 45 down through verse number 52.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: mark chapter number six. And specifically this evening, we'll be looking at verses 45 down through verse number 52.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: so the men sat down and number about 5,000
point_summary: The feeding of the five thousand, cited as backdrop, frames Jesus as Creator and Lord over nature, whose willingness to be received—never forced—meets human need on its own terms.

context: they supposed it had been a spirit and cried out for they all saw him and were troubled
point_summary: When the disciples saw Jesus walking on the sea, they supposed him to be a spirit and cried out in terror—a fear Jesus immediately addressed with the reassurance "It is I, be not afraid."

context: Immediately he talked with them and saith unto them, be of good cheer. It is I, be not afraid.
point_summary: Christ's words "Be of good cheer; it is I; be not afraid" directly address the disciples' terror, identified in the text as literal fright at seeing him walk on the sea.

context: it says to us in verse number 51 of Mark 6, that he went up unto them into the ship and the wind ceased
point_summary: Christ's entry into the ship in verse 51 is presented as the decisive moment when the storm ceased, illustrating his sovereign power over nature and his personal intervention for those who willingly receive him.

context: these disciples in Mark chapter number 6, they got to see a great miracle. They saw that the wind ceased when he came up in the ship.
point_summary: Christ's omniscient awareness of the disciples' storm in Mark 6 grounds the assurance that He sees every believer's individual trial, knows their precise need, and has the power to meet it.

context: Go ye into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature.
point_summary: God has ordained that saved people—not stars, rocks, or angels—are the appointed messengers to carry the gospel to every creature, making personal witness an inescapable obligation.

context: go and preach the gospel to every creature
point_summary: Receiving the gospel creates a responsibility to actively proclaim it to every creature, since the new covenant's blessings are not truths to be held privately but sent outward to a lost world.

context: the angel, as they make the statement to those that were there, in Mark 16 verse number 17 says, tell his disciples and Peter.
point_summary: The angel's singling out of Peter by name in Mark 16:7 illuminates Paul's deliberate, personal charge to Archippus—both are direct, individual calls to a discouraged servant who needed specific address.

context: the Bible describes to us the miracle of the man that was born of four
point_summary: The crowd that blocked Zacchaeus parallels the crowd in Mark 2 that prevented the paralytic's friends from reaching Jesus, illustrating that pressing multitudes repeatedly hindered access to Christ.

context: Mark simply says, Preach the gospel to every creature.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And I want you to just briefly look at Mark 5, just for a moment this morning.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The man that we read of in Mark chapter number 5, we're familiar with the passage of Scripture. I won't take time to develop all of it, but in this passage of Scripture, it's the maniac from Gadara.
point_summary: The Gadarene demoniac's transformation—from demon-possessed to clothed, in his right mind, and sitting at Jesus' feet—illustrates that salvation is the greatest experience a person can have, compelling witness to others.

context: and they come to Jesus and see him that was possessed with the devil and had the legion sitting and clothed in his right mind, and they were afraid.
point_summary: The transformed demoniac—now clothed, in his right mind, and sitting at Jesus' feet—is presented as a picture of salvation's power to make a person a new creature.

context: Albeit Jesus suffered him not, but saith unto him. So Jesus didn't allow him to get on the ship and to go to the other side. He said, go home to thy friends and tell them how great things the Lord hath done for thee and have had compassion on thee.
point_summary: Rather than allowing the healed demoniac to follow Him, Jesus redirected his gratitude into witness, commanding him to go home and tell others what God had done—illustrating that salvation issues in a personal, immediate call to go and tell.

context: The Bible tells us in verse number 20, and he departed and began to publish in Decapolis how great things Jesus had done for him and all men did marvel.
point_summary: Obedience to Christ's command to "go and tell" is illustrated in the formerly demon-possessed man who, rather than following Jesus, departed and proclaimed throughout Decapolis what great things Jesus had done for him.

context: as we look at Mark 5 and use it as our theme this year for our missions conference
point_summary: The healed demoniac's commission in Mark 5—"go home to thy friends and tell them"—is held up as the missions conference theme, grounding the church's evangelistic responsibility in Christ's own direct command to go and tell.

context: as you read Matthew Mark Luke and John and you begin to read about the life and Ministry of the Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: now as he walked by the Sea of Galilee, he saw Simon and Andrew his brother casting a net into the sea, and they were fishers.
point_summary: Christ's deliberate movement to the Sea of Galilee illustrates that he walked with divine purpose, knowing exactly where he was going and whom he would call.

context: go ye into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature
point_summary: The Great Commission's command to preach the gospel to every creature defines the believer's overarching purpose, expressed not only in formal ministry but wherever God places them in daily life.

context: We looked at Mark one, when he purposefully walked along the sea so that he could call Peter and Andrew.
point_summary: Christ's deliberate walk along the sea to call Peter and Andrew illustrates that bearing "much fruit" as his disciples means purposefully pursuing the lost as fishers of men.

context: In Mark chapter 10, the Bible says this, in verse number 41, it says, and when the ten heard it,
point_summary: Amid the disciples' dispute over positions of honor, Christ's response to James and John's request serves as evidence that Jesus walked humbly, modeling the humility believers are called to imitate.

context: James and John, the sons of Zebedee, came unto him saying, Master, we would that thou shouldest do for us whatsoever we shall desire.
point_summary: Mark 10:35 introduces the request of James and John, used as context for the sermon's point that Jesus responded to self-seeking ambition by teaching servant-greatness, illustrating his humility.

context: And Jesus says, what would you that I should do for you?
point_summary: Mark 10:36 is cited within a passage used to illustrate Christ's humility, contrasting the disciples' ambition for positions of glory with Jesus's teaching that greatness means servanthood.

context: They said unto him, grant us that we may sit one on thy right hand and the other on thy left in thy glory.
point_summary: James and John's request for seats of honor (v. 37) sets up Jesus' counter-teaching in vv. 42–44, used here as evidence that Christ walked humbly by defining greatness as servanthood rather than position.

context: The ten heard what James and John were discussing and they were much displeased.
point_summary: Mark 10:41–45 is introduced as evidence that Jesus walked humbly, with the disciples' dispute over greatness serving as the occasion for Christ's teaching that true greatness means serving rather than exercising lordship.

context: Jesus called them to him and saith unto them, ye know not that ye know, excuse me, ye know that they which are accounted to rule over the Gentiles exercise lordship over them.
point_summary: Mark 10:42 is cited to demonstrate Christ's humility, contrasting worldly lordship and authority-seeking with his teaching that greatness among his followers means serving as a minister.

context: For even the Son of Man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, to give his life a ransom for many.
point_summary: Greatness in Christ's kingdom is defined not by status or recognition but by servanthood, as Jesus himself modeled by coming not to be served but to minister and give his life.

context: Look over at Mark's gospel, if you will. Look at Mark 16. We find in Mark 16, again, verses 1-8
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Look over at Mark's gospel, if you will. Look at Mark 16. We find in Mark 16, again, verses 1-8
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Look over at Mark's gospel, if you will. Look at Mark 16. We find in Mark 16, again, verses 1-8
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Look over at Mark's gospel, if you will. Look at Mark 16. We find in Mark 16, again, verses 1-8
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Look over at Mark's gospel, if you will. Look at Mark 16. We find in Mark 16, again, verses 1-8
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Look over at Mark's gospel, if you will. Look at Mark 16. We find in Mark 16, again, verses 1-8
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Look over at Mark's gospel, if you will. Look at Mark 16. We find in Mark 16, again, verses 1-8
point_summary: The specific mention of Peter by name in the resurrection announcement signals his restoration after denial, distinguishing him from the other scattered disciples.

context: Look over at Mark's gospel, if you will. Look at Mark 16. We find in Mark 16, again, verses 1-8
point_summary: The disciples' fear and trembling at the empty tomb, recorded in Mark 16:8, illustrates the emotional devastation of the saddened followers before resurrection hope broke through.

context: go your way, tell his disciples and Peter
point_summary: The angelic command to tell "his disciples and Peter" serves as the backdrop for establishing that the disciples were scattered, saddened, and mourning—setting up the emotional state into which the resurrection news would come.

context: Now when Jesus was risen early the first day of the week, he appeared first to Mary Magdalene out of whom he had cast seven devils
point_summary: Mark 16:9's notation that the risen Christ appeared first to Mary Magdalene while the disciples mourned and wept establishes the emotional state of grief among those who had witnessed the crucifixion before resurrection news reached them.

context: where the Bible says in Mark 16 and verse 20, and they went forth and preached everywhere, the Lord working with them and confirming the word with signs following. Amen.
point_summary: Signs confirmed the apostolic word during the foundational era of the church, when hearers lacked a completed Scripture to verify the message being preached.

context: And they come again to Jerusalem, and as he was walking in the temple, there come to him the chief priests and the scribes and the elders.
point_summary: Jesus' courageous walk is illustrated by his continued, unhesitating presence in the temple before hostile religious leaders who were actively seeking to kill him.

context: By what authority doest thou these things? And who gave thee this authority to do these things?
point_summary: Jesus' willingness to walk openly in the temple—confronted by hostile religious leaders seeking to kill him—is presented as the defining example of courageous walking that believers are called to emulate.

context: we find that they take this approach in Mark 11, and they say, By what authority doest thou these things?
point_summary: Near the end of his earthly ministry, Jesus walked openly in the temple among leaders actively seeking to kill him, demonstrating that obedience to God's purpose requires courageous walking even under hostile interrogation.

context: And Jesus answered and said unto them, I will also ask of you one question, and answer me, and I will tell you by what authority I do these things
point_summary: Facing hostile religious authorities who demanded to know his credentials, Jesus responded not with deference but with a counter-question, demonstrating the courage and wisdom with which he walked among his enemies.

context: The baptism of John, was it from heaven, or of men? Answer me
point_summary: Jesus' counter-question about John's baptism—demanding they answer him first—illustrates his wisdom and courage in refusing to be cornered by hostile authorities who wielded power over others.

context: But if we shall say of men, they feared the people. For all men count it John, that he was a prophet indeed.
point_summary: Jesus's opponents refused to answer his question about John's baptism because either answer would publicly expose them—leaving them trapped by their own political calculation and fear of the people.

context: And they answered and said unto Jesus, We cannot tell. We cannot tell. And notice what Jesus said, Neither do I tell you by what authority I do these things.
point_summary: Jesus's refusal to answer the religious leaders—mirroring their refusal to answer him—is presented as a display of divine courage before earthly authority.

context: He said that we're to go into all the world and we're to preach the gospel to every creature.
point_summary: The Great Commission's mandate to go into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature is presented as the foundational missionary charge Christ gave before His ascension.

context: The Bible tells us in Mark's gospel in chapter 16 and verse number 1 that they went there to anoint the body of Jesus.
point_summary: The women's journey to the tomb was driven by an unfinished burial rite—anointing the body—which Mark 16:1 identifies as their explicit purpose, explaining why they carried spices and made the trip at all.

context: Mark 16 and verse number 3 provide some interesting insight with regard to the conversation between Mary Magdalene and the other Mary as they're approaching the tomb.
point_summary: The women's question—"Who shall roll us away the stone?"—reveals that their minds were fixed on practical obstacles, with no expectation of resurrection, even as they carried spices to anoint a body they believed remained in the tomb.

context: Mark 16 and verse number 3 tells us that there's this question that is raised, who shall roll us away the stone?
point_summary: The women's question — "Who shall roll us away the stone?" — reveals they approached the tomb with genuine purpose but no solution to the obstacle standing between them and the body of Jesus.

context: tonight, Mark chapter number nine. It's a passage of scripture that really the first portion of Mark chapter nine begins with the record of the Mount of Transfiguration.
point_summary: Mark 9's account of the Transfiguration is presented as a foundational event of overwhelming divine impact, one Peter himself later invoked to authenticate the sure word of prophecy.

context: Jesus comes and He says in verse number 16, what question you with them?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Turn back, keep your place here in Mark 9, but go back to Mark chapter number 6 if you will.
point_summary: The disciples' inability to cast out the dumb spirit in Mark 9 is set in sharp contrast to the authority over unclean spirits they had previously been given and exercised in Mark 6.

context: In Mark chapter number 6, the Lord gives us some interesting insight as to the work of his servants, the work of his disciples. In verse number 7, notice what the Bible says, and he, Jesus, called unto him the twelve.
point_summary: The disciples' inability in Mark 9 stands in sharp contrast to the authority over unclean spirits Jesus had explicitly granted them in Mark 6:7, raising the question of what changed.

context: Verse number 8, and commanded them that they should take nothing for their journey, save a staff only, no strip, no bread, no money in their purse, but be shod with sandals
point_summary: Mark 6:8 is cited as background evidence that the disciples had previously been granted power over unclean spirits and sent out successfully, making their later failure in Mark 9 all the more striking.

context: Jesus will say in verse number 19, when he says, oh, faithless generation.
point_summary: Jesus's rebuke of "faithless generation" in Mark 9:19 is directed at the disciples, not the suffering father, exposing how prior success in ministry can breed self-reliance that replaces dependence on God.

context: in Mark chapter number 6, they went out and God gave them power, and they cast out some devils
point_summary: The disciples' earlier success in Mark 6 casting out demons carried a danger: that they would begin to trust in their own ability rather than continuing to depend on God's power.

context: He answereth him and saith, O faithless generation, how long shall I be with you? How long shall I suffer you? Bring him unto me.
point_summary: Jesus's rebuke — "O faithless generation" — is read as disappointment directed at the disciples specifically, who had repeatedly witnessed His power yet still failed to trust Him.

context: Jesus said unto him, if thou canst believe, all things are possible to him that believeth.
point_summary: Jesus's redirection of the father's conditional "if thou canst" back onto the man as "if thou canst believe" establishes that God's ability is unlimited, and the only variable is the believer's faith.

context: the father of the child cried out and said with tears, Lord, I believe! Help thou mine unbelief.
point_summary: The desperate father's tearful cry — affirming belief while pleading for help with his unbelief — illustrates that faith can coexist with doubt, and that honest prayer for greater faith is itself an act of trust in God's ability.

context: And Jesus said unto him, If thou canst believe, all things are possible to him that believeth.
point_summary: Faith in God's omnipotence is the condition Jesus sets for the impossible becoming possible, as the father's appeal meets the promise that all things are possible to him that believeth.

context: When Jesus saw that the people came running together, He rebuked the foul spirit saying unto Him, Thou dumb and deaf spirit, I charge thee, come out of him and enter no more into him.
point_summary: Christ's direct command over the unclean spirit in Mark 9:25 demonstrates that God holds absolute authority and power over Satan and all evil spirits.

context: And the spirit cried and rent him sore and came out of him, and he was as one dead, insomuch that many said, He is dead.
point_summary: Mark 9:26's violent exit of the demon and Jesus's restoring touch demonstrate that God holds sovereign authority and power over Satan and all evil spirits.

context: But Jesus took him by the hand and lifted him up and he arose.
point_summary: Mark 9:27 illustrates that God holds absolute authority over Satan and all evil spirits, demonstrated in Christ physically lifting the delivered boy to life.

context: And he said unto them, This kind can come forth by nothing, but by prayer and fasting.
point_summary: Spiritual self-sufficiency undermines prayer, and Jesus' words in Mark 9:29 expose this by tying the disciples' failure directly to their neglect of prayer and fasting.

context: We thank You for this interaction, this account that You've given us of Mark 9. Lord, we thank You for demonstrating Your power over this unclean spirit and healing this boy, this son.
point_summary: Specific prayer, modeled by the disciples' inquiry in Mark 9, is presented as the means by which faith grows as believers witness God's direct and particular answers.

context: Mark records for us in Mark chapter number 12 in verse number 24, this truth again with regard to preservation.
point_summary: Christ's rebuke of the Sadducees in Mark 12:24 is presented as a declaration of the truth of preservation, set within a confrontation where his opponents did not believe in the resurrection.

context: go and preach the Gospel to every creature
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: go and preach the gospel to every creature
context: Because Jesus said, go and preach the gospel to every creature
point_summary: The Great Commission's command to preach the gospel to every creature carries both a unique privilege and an inescapable personal responsibility, because the gospel is confrontational and the only cure for humanity's heart problem is Jesus.

context: Mark chapter number 12 this evening, and I'll be looking at verses 18-27
point_summary: Mark 12:18 introduces the Sadducees as religious leaders who approached Jesus not in genuine inquiry but with a calculated question designed to corner him.

context: Mark chapter number 12 this evening, and I'll be looking at verses 18-27
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: ...and Jesus answering said unto them, Do ye not therefore err, because ye know not the Scriptures, neither the power of God.
point_summary: Ignorance of Scripture and of God's power are identified as the twin roots of serious error, with the Greek word for "err" in verse 24 being the same word elsewhere translated "deceive."

context: ...he is not the God of the dead, but the God of the living.
point_summary: The word translated "err" in Mark 12:27 is the same Greek word rendered "deceive" or "deceived" elsewhere, revealing that the Sadducees' error about resurrection was a form of self-deception rooted in ignorance of Scripture.

context: ...then come unto him the Sadducees, which say, there is no resurrection. And they asked him, saying...
point_summary: The Sadducees' denial of resurrection, stated plainly in verse 18, sets the stage for examining what led them into the deception Jesus directly charges them with.

context: Master, Moses wrote unto us, if a man's brother die and leave his wife behind him, and leave no children, that his brother should take his wife and raise up seed unto his brother.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: ...now there were seven brethren, and the first took a wife, and dying left no seed.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And the second took her, and died, neither left he any seed. And the third likewise.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And the seven had her, and left no seed. Last of all, the woman died also.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: In the resurrection therefore, when they shall rise, whose wife shall she be of them? For the seven had heard a wife.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And Jesus says, do you not therefore err? Number one, because you know not the Scriptures. Number two, because you know not the power of God.
point_summary: Jesus's rebuke in Mark 12:24 identifies two inseparable errors—ignorance of Scripture and ignorance of God's power—with the sermon pressing that no circumstance lies beyond the reach of the God who creates, plagues Egypt, and raises the dead.

reference_text: He is not the God of the dead, but the God of the living
context: He is not the God of the dead, but the God of the living
point_summary: Jesus's appeal to Exodus 3—where God identifies himself as the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob long after their physical deaths—demonstrates that the patriarchs must still be living, proving resurrection from Scripture the Sadducees claimed to accept.

context: But shall it profit a man that gain the whole world but lose his own soul? Is that not what Jesus asked?
point_summary: Cited to contrast worldly gain with the soul's eternal worth, underscoring why Christ's atoning transaction—not earthly profit—is the only sufficient remedy for sin's consequence.

context: Go ye into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature
point_summary: The Great Commission's explicit inclusion of Samaria in Acts 1:8 demonstrates that God's command to preach the gospel is all-inclusive, leaving no people group—even despised ones—conveniently skipped.

context: we've been given the responsibility to preach the gospel to every creature. To go into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature
point_summary: The Great Commission mandate to preach the gospel to every creature is presented as the church's living memorial, replacing the stone monuments of Joshua 4 with a continuing witness to Christ's death, burial, and resurrection.

context: preach the gospel to every creature
point_summary: The urgency of Christ's return motivates faithful obedience to the Great Commission mandate to preach the gospel to every creature before it is too late.

context: he came to minister not to be ministered unto
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jesus had told them go ye into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature
