point_summary: Prayer is indispensable to the church's mission, cited here as the command Christ gave—go and preach to every creature—which the early believers upheld through united, persistent prayer even under persecution.

context: they said unto her thou art mad
point_summary: Prayer can coexist with weak faith, as the gathered believers who had been interceding for Peter dismissed Rhoda's report as madness rather than believing God had answered them.

context: if thou can't believe all things are possible To them that believe
point_summary: Faith and doubt can coexist in the same praying community, as illustrated by believers who prayed for Peter yet called Rhoda "mad" when their prayer was answered.

context: I believe help thou mine unbelief
point_summary: Faith and doubt can coexist in the same praying community, as illustrated by believers who prayed for Peter yet struggled to believe the answer had arrived.

context: go ye therefore teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, the Son, the Holy Ghost, teaching them to observe all things whatsoever I commanded you
point_summary: The Great Commission's daunting global scope is met by Christ's emphatic "lo"—a word designed to arrest attention—assuring believers that His presence accompanies them in fulfilling it.

context: Mark chapter 16, in verse number 20, the Bible says that they went forth, that's the disciples, the apostles
point_summary: Miracles in the apostolic era served as confirming signs that drew crowds and opened ears to the gospel, not as ends in themselves.

reference_text: Go ye into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature
context: Go ye into all the world and preach the gospel, to what? To every creature
point_summary: The universal scope of Christ's redemption — purchasing people from every kindred, tongue, and nation — demands a corresponding universal mission, grounded in the command to preach the gospel to every creature.

context: we are commanded to go to all creatures
point_summary: The present-day responsibility of the redeemed, while awaiting heavenly worship, is to obey Christ's command to preach the gospel to every creature without keeping it to themselves.

context: proclaim the gospel to every creature
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: going and preaching the gospel to every creature
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: what shall it profit a man if he gained the whole world, but lose his own soul
point_summary: Gaining the whole world holds no ultimate profit if a person loses their soul, since every individual—rich or poor—will one day stand before God and be judged on eternal, not financial, standing.

context: Go into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature.
point_summary: Every believer bears responsibility to proclaim the gospel, since Romans 10's chain of logic—calling, believing, hearing, preaching, being sent—shows that reaching the lost requires Christians to go into all the world as ambassadors.

context: he was sitting, he was clothed, he was sitting in his right mind at the feet of Jesus
point_summary: The demoniac of Gadara, once delivered, is pictured sitting clothed and in his right mind at Jesus' feet—held up as a model of the posture of hearing that Mary exemplified.

context: come apart
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: it says that we're to go and preach the gospel to every creature
point_summary: Every believer is called to preach the gospel (Mark 16:15), but that universal obligation does not require holding the office of pastor.

context: they were toiling and rowing, the disciples
point_summary: Divine providence over natural forces is illustrated through the disciples' contrary wind in Mark 6:48, showing that God can redirect circumstances that resist human effort and striving.

context: the wind was contrary
point_summary: Divine sovereignty over wind and direction illustrates that true prayer surrenders to God's leading rather than seeking his approval on a self-chosen path, as the disciples' contrary wind in Mark 6:48 pictures.

context: the woman that was dealing with the issue of blood for 12 years. She spent all of her substance to try to get healed, and she heard about Jesus. The people were thronging Him, and she said, if I could just touch the hem of His garment. He says, who touched me? Peter's like, what do you mean, who touched you? There's all kinds of people that are thronging you. Virtue has departed. Your faith has made you whole.
point_summary: The woman with the issue of blood, having exhausted every human remedy over twelve years, illustrates that true deliverance comes only when trouble drives us to call upon God alone.

context: the disciples instructed to pass on to the other side
point_summary: Trials are not designed to destroy but to develop, as seen when Christ allowed the storm the disciples faced in order to reveal his power to calm it.
